questions.question: The task description says submitProbabilityEdit posts to /v1/markets/{id}/conditional-edit, but the actual code posts to /v1/markets/{id}/orders/probability-edit. Similarly, submitEventTrade is described as posting to /v1/markets/{id}/trade-event but actually posts to /v1/markets/{id}/orders/event-trade, and getServiceIndex is described as calling /v1 but actually calls /. Should tests match the actual code (not the task description)?
questions.why_it_matters: If tests are written against the task description endpoints instead of actual code endpoints, every test for these three functions will assert the wrong URL and give false confidence.

questions.question: The existing tests use minimal response stubs (e.g. { market: { id: 'm1' }, meta: {...} }) rather than full type-conformant objects. Should new tests follow this same minimal stub pattern, or should they use more complete response fixtures matching the TypeScript interfaces?
questions.why_it_matters: Determines the verbosity and maintenance burden of each new test. Minimal stubs are faster to write and easier to maintain; full fixtures catch more type issues but add bulk.

questions.question: For functions that accept optional pagination params (getMarketEvents, getMarketComments with fromSeq/limit), should we add separate tests for the no-params case AND the with-params case (like the existing getMarketAnalytics interval tests), or is one test per function sufficient?
questions.why_it_matters: getMarketEvents and getMarketComments have identical pagination logic with query string construction. Testing both param and no-param cases doubles coverage but may be redundant given the pattern is already proven by getMarketAnalytics tests.

questions.question: For POST functions requiring a session (submitProbabilityEdit, resolveMarket, submitEventTrade, submitMarketComment), should tests verify that the X-Bayes-Agent-Id header is set correctly from session.agentId, or just verify the URL and HTTP method?
questions.why_it_matters: The session header is a critical auth mechanism. Testing it ensures authenticated endpoints cannot silently regress to sending unauthenticated requests. However, the existing tests don't cover any session-bearing functions yet, so there's no established pattern.

questions.question: createMarket accepts an optional session parameter but is a POST. Should the test cover both the with-session and without-session cases, or just one?
questions.why_it_matters: createMarket is unique among POST functions in that session is optional. Testing both paths ensures the Content-Type header is always set for POSTs regardless of session presence.

assumptions.assumption: Tests should be written against the actual code endpoints, not the task description endpoints (which contain inaccuracies).
assumptions.if_wrong: Tests would assert wrong URLs and all would pass vacuously against mocks but not reflect real behavior.

assumptions.assumption: Follow the existing minimal stub pattern for response data — just enough fields to prove the function returns the response.
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to create full fixture objects matching every field of the TypeScript response interfaces.

assumptions.assumption: POST functions should be tested with assertions on method, URL, body (JSON.stringify of payload), and Content-Type header.
assumptions.if_wrong: Tests would miss verifying that the request function correctly sets Content-Type for POST methods.

assumptions.assumption: Session-bearing functions should verify the X-Bayes-Agent-Id header is passed through.
assumptions.if_wrong: Auth header regression would go undetected by these unit tests.

assumptions.assumption: Functions with optional query params (getMarketEvents, getMarketComments) should have two tests each: one without params and one with params, following the getMarketAnalytics pattern.
assumptions.if_wrong: Could reduce to a single test per function if the team prefers minimal test count.

assumptions.assumption: No new test utilities or helpers are needed — the existing mockFetch + jsonResponse pattern is sufficient for all 10 new functions.
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need a session mock factory or response fixture builder.
